summary_text: consulted_worldview_source: /Users/masayuki/.claude/docs/philosophy.md consulted_paths: /Users/masayuki/.claude/CODEX.md, /Users/masayuki/.codex/skills/slide-script-prep/SKILL.md, /Users/masayuki/.claude/docs/philosophy.md used_obsidian_for_worldview: no 1. core_thesis: Responsible AI adoption in medical contexts works only when AI is introduced as accountable clinical infrastructure that strengthens human judgment rather than replacing it. 2. slide_outline: - Medicine is a special case because error costs are high, trust is fragile, and decision accountability cannot be outsourced. - The core mistake in AI adoption is treating model capability as the main problem when the real bottleneck is workflow, context, and institutional memory. - Responsible adoption starts by defining the human decision boundary, audit trail, evidence path, and escalation rule for every use case. - Early wins should come from assistive, low-risk deployments that reduce clerical burden while keeping clinical reasoning visible. - Evaluation must go beyond accuracy to include explainability, challengeability, traceability, and operational fit inside medical practice. - The leadership agenda is to invest in governance, domain translation, and cognition infrastructure so trust scales with adoption.
